title: Digital Colonialism
summary: An academic and a discussant examine the concept of digital colonialism — the concentration of digital infrastructure, data flows, and platform power in a small number of wealthy nations and corporations — and debate its implications for sovereignty, labour, and development in the Global South.

body.speaker: Academic
body.text: I want to begin with what I think is an underappreciated structural feature of the contemporary internet: the degree to which its physical and commercial infrastructure is concentrated in a handful of states and corporations. When we talk about digital connectivity as a democratising force, we tend to focus on endpoints — the billions of people who now have access to the internet — and ignore the architecture of control that sits between those endpoints and the content they access.

body.speaker: Discussant
body.text: That's a useful corrective. The narrative of the internet as a flat, decentralised network was always more mythological than accurate, but it has become increasingly misleading as platform consolidation has accelerated. We are now in a situation where a small number of hyperscale cloud providers host the majority of global internet traffic, and a similarly small number of platforms mediate the vast majority of human communication, commerce, and cultural production.

body.speaker: Academic
body.text: The term 'digital colonialism' has attracted both enthusiastic adoption and significant scepticism. Critics of the term argue that it trivialises the history of material colonial violence. Proponents argue that the term is apt because the structural dynamics — extraction of value from peripheral regions to core ones, dependency relationships, suppression of local alternatives — are genuinely analogous. I find the concept analytically useful even while acknowledging its limitations.

body.speaker: Discussant
body.text: The extraction argument is the most concrete one. When a user in Kenya or Indonesia engages with a social media platform or a search engine, they generate data — behavioural data, preference data, relational data — that is processed and monetised by corporations headquartered in California or, to a lesser extent, in Beijing. The value created by that engagement largely does not return to the community that generated it.

body.speaker: Academic
body.text: And the asymmetry goes deeper than data extraction. The terms of service, content moderation policies, and algorithmic curation systems that govern the digital experience of a user in Lagos or Manila are designed for a different context — primarily for regulatory environments and cultural sensibilities in the United States or Europe. Users in other regions are subject to governance frameworks they did not shape and cannot meaningfully contest.

body.speaker: Discussant
body.text: Content moderation is a particularly acute case. There have been well-documented instances where content in African languages has been moderated — or not moderated — in ways that reflect the resource allocation decisions of platforms that invest heavily in English-language moderation and much less in Swahili, Amharic, or Yoruba. The consequences in contexts of political violence or electoral disinformation can be severe.

body.speaker: Academic
body.text: The infrastructural dimension is equally important and perhaps more durable. The subsea cable networks that carry the bulk of international internet traffic are largely owned by consortia that include the major platform companies. A government seeking to exercise genuine digital sovereignty faces the difficulty that the infrastructure of its citizens' digital lives is physically located elsewhere and governed by foreign law.

body.speaker: Discussant
body.text: China's model is sometimes invoked here as an alternative — a state that has built its own parallel internet infrastructure and enforces its own governance norms. But I think we need to be careful about treating Chinese internet sovereignty as a model for the Global South, because it is sovereignty exercised in the service of authoritarian control.

body.speaker: Academic
body.text: The Internet Governance Forum and similar multi-stakeholder bodies have produced valuable dialogue but limited enforceable outcomes. Proposals for a digital trade agreement that addresses data localisation, algorithmic accountability, and the taxation of digital rents have stalled in the face of opposition from incumbent platform corporations.

body.speaker: Discussant
body.text: The large language models and foundation models that are reshaping professional and creative labour globally are trained predominantly on data from a small number of languages and cultural contexts. Their outputs carry embedded assumptions about what constitutes good writing, valid reasoning, and appropriate expression — assumptions rooted in the dominant cultural contexts of the training data.

body.speaker: Academic
body.text: The annotation, content filtering, and data labelling work that makes large AI models functional is disproportionately performed by workers in Kenya, the Philippines, and similar labour markets, under conditions that have attracted significant criticism from labour researchers. The value chain of AI is global; the rewards are not.

body.speaker: Discussant
body.text: Given all of this, what does a serious policy response look like? I have seen proposals ranging from data sovereignty frameworks to mandatory open licensing of AI models trained on public data, to reformed international tax arrangements for digital services.

body.speaker: Academic
body.text: My own view is that the most promising avenue is regional cooperation, building shared digital infrastructure at a scale that creates genuine negotiating leverage with incumbent platforms. The underlying logic is that no individual state in the Global South has sufficient market size to impose conditions on hyperscale platforms, but a coordinated regional bloc might.

body.speaker: Discussant
body.text: That logic is sound, but it requires a level of institutional trust and political coordination that is genuinely difficult to sustain. None of this should lead us to despair, but it should lead us to be honest about the scale of what we are asking for.

annotations.meaningVi: nhà cung cấp đám mây siêu quy mô
annotations.exampleEn: Hyperscale cloud providers like AWS and Google Cloud host the majority of the world's internet services.

annotations.meaningVi: sự hợp nhất của các nền tảng
annotations.exampleEn: Platform consolidation has reduced the number of major social media companies to a handful.

annotations.meaningVi: chủ nghĩa thực dân kỹ thuật số
annotations.exampleEn: Critics argue that digital colonialism extracts data value from developing nations to enrich tech companies elsewhere.

annotations.meaningVi: khai thác dữ liệu
annotations.exampleEn: Data extraction by platforms has been compared to the resource extraction of historical colonialism.

annotations.meaningVi: được kiếm tiền từ, được thương mại hóa
annotations.exampleEn: User data is collected and monetised through targeted advertising.

annotations.meaningVi: sắp xếp nội dung bằng thuật toán
annotations.exampleEn: Algorithmic curation determines which posts appear in your social media feed.

annotations.meaningVi: kiểm duyệt nội dung
annotations.exampleEn: Content moderation policies vary wildly across platforms and rarely account for non-English contexts.

annotations.meaningVi: thông tin sai lệch trong bầu cử
annotations.exampleEn: Electoral disinformation on social media has influenced the outcome of several national elections.

annotations.meaningVi: mạng cáp biển
annotations.exampleEn: Subsea cable networks carry over 95% of international internet traffic.

annotations.meaningVi: chủ quyền kỹ thuật số
annotations.exampleEn: Digital sovereignty means the ability of a state to control its own digital infrastructure and data.

annotations.meaningVi: các tổ chức đa bên liên quan
annotations.exampleEn: Multi-stakeholder bodies bring together governments, civil society, and the private sector to shape internet governance.

annotations.meaningVi: bản địa hóa dữ liệu
annotations.exampleEn: Data localisation laws require that certain data about citizens be stored within the country.

annotations.meaningVi: trách nhiệm giải trình về thuật toán
annotations.exampleEn: Algorithmic accountability requires that companies explain how their systems make consequential decisions.

annotations.meaningVi: mô hình nền tảng (AI)
annotations.exampleEn: Foundation models like GPT and Gemini are trained on vast datasets and fine-tuned for specific applications.

annotations.meaningVi: gán nhãn dữ liệu
annotations.exampleEn: Data labelling involves humans annotating training examples so AI models can learn from them.

annotations.meaningVi: đòn bẩy đàm phán
annotations.exampleEn: A regional bloc creates negotiating leverage that no individual country could achieve alone.

annotations.meaningVi: khối khu vực
annotations.exampleEn: The European Union operates as a regional bloc with significant power to regulate global tech companies.

annotations.meaningVi: các nền tảng đương nhiệm (đang thống trị)
annotations.exampleEn: Incumbent platforms resist regulation that would undermine their dominant market positions.

annotations.meaningVi: địa tô kỹ thuật số
annotations.exampleEn: Digital rents are the excess profits extracted by platform monopolies from users and content creators.

annotations.meaningVi: các vùng ngoại vi (trong quan hệ kinh tế toàn cầu)
annotations.exampleEn: In dependency theory, peripheral regions supply resources and labour that enrich core nations.

grammarNotes.title: Reporting Verbs that Signal Academic Stance
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn học thuật, động từ tường thuật (reporting verbs) không chỉ đơn thuần giới thiệu nội dung mà còn thể hiện thái độ của người nói. 'Critics argue' (lập luận có tính phê phán), 'proponents contend' (những người ủng hộ khẳng định), 'I find X useful' (phán xét cá nhân thận trọng). Việc lựa chọn động từ tường thuật phân biệt người nói đang trình bày, đánh giá, hay bác bỏ một quan điểm. Ví dụ: 'Critics of the term argue that it trivialises...' cho thấy đây là quan điểm tranh cãi, không phải sự thật được thừa nhận.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Reporting verbs in academic discourse signal whether the speaker endorses, attributes, or distances from a claim. 'Argue' attributes a view without endorsing it; 'contend' implies the claim is contested; 'acknowledge' signals concession. 'I find the concept analytically useful even while acknowledging its limitations' expresses calibrated personal endorsement. Choosing the right reporting verb is essential for precise academic voice.

grammarNotes.title: Cleft and Pseudo-Cleft Constructions for Topicalisation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc tách câu (cleft: 'It is X that...') và giả tách câu (pseudo-cleft: 'What X is/does is...') được dùng để đặt tiêu điểm thông tin vào một yếu tố cụ thể. Trong bài: 'The value created by that engagement largely does not return to the community that generated it. The user receives a service; the platform receives a commodity.' Dấu chấm phẩy song song nhấn mạnh sự đối lập. Ngoài ra: 'What most states actually want is something different' — cấu trúc giả tách câu nhấn mạnh nội dung mệnh đề bổ ngữ. Những cấu trúc này rất hữu ích trong tranh luận để làm nổi bật điểm quan trọng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Pseudo-cleft constructions ('What X wants/needs/does is...') topicalise the predicate by placing it in focus position at the end of the sentence. 'What most states actually want is something different: genuine participation in governance.' This contrasts with a plain sentence ('Most states want genuine participation') by emphasising the predicate as the informational climax. Similarly, 'The value chain of AI is global; the rewards are not' uses syntactic parallelism and ellipsis for emphasis.

translationVi: Học giả mở đầu bằng cách chỉ ra rằng cơ sở hạ tầng internet tập trung trong tay một số ít quốc gia và tập đoàn, trong khi diễn ngôn về kết nối kỹ thuật số như một lực lượng dân chủ hóa bỏ qua kiến trúc kiểm soát đó. Người thảo luận đồng ý rằng sự hợp nhất nền tảng đã làm cho khái niệm 'mạng phẳng phi tập trung' ngày càng sai lệch. Hai người xem xét thuật ngữ 'chủ nghĩa thực dân kỹ thuật số': các nhà phê bình cho rằng nó hạ thấp bạo lực thực dân lịch sử; những người ủng hộ cho rằng nó phù hợp vì các động lực cấu trúc tương tự — khai thác giá trị từ ngoại vi về trung tâm. Dữ liệu của người dùng ở Kenya hoặc Indonesia được tập đoàn ở California kiếm tiền từ. Người dùng ở Lagos phải tuân theo các khung quản trị được thiết kế cho bối cảnh Mỹ hoặc châu Âu. Kiểm duyệt nội dung trong tiếng Swahili hay Yoruba nhận được đầu tư ít hơn nhiều, với hậu quả nghiêm trọng trong các tình huống bạo lực chính trị. Cơ sở hạ tầng cáp biển và các mô hình AI có cùng vấn đề tập trung. AI được đào tạo trên dữ liệu thiếu đa dạng văn hóa, trong khi lao động gán nhãn dữ liệu diễn ra ở Kenya và Philippines với điều kiện bị chỉ trích. Phản ứng chính sách hứa hẹn nhất là hợp tác khu vực xây dựng cơ sở hạ tầng chung để tạo đòn bẩy đàm phán, dù điều này đòi hỏi sự phối hợp thể chế khó duy trì.
criticalThinkingQuestion: The academic argues that regional cooperation offers the most promising policy response to digital colonialism. But the discussant points out that even the European Union — with far greater economic weight — has struggled to enforce its digital regulations at pace. Given this, should activists focus on reforming global internet governance institutions instead? What are the trade-offs between regional and global approaches?

questions.prompt: What structural feature of the internet does the academic identify as underappreciated?
options: The speed at which internet infrastructure is being built in developing countries
The degree to which internet infrastructure is concentrated in a handful of states and corporations
The vulnerability of internet infrastructure to cyberattacks
The cost of internet access for users in the Global South
questions.explanation: 'The degree to which its physical and commercial infrastructure is concentrated in a handful of states and corporations.'
questions.hint: The academic contrasts endpoints with the architecture between them.

questions.prompt: How does the discussant describe the narrative of the internet as a flat, decentralised network?
options: Accurate in the early days but now increasingly valid again due to decentralised technologies
Always more mythological than accurate, and now increasingly misleading as consolidation accelerates
An accurate description of the technical architecture but not of its commercial control
Partially true for developed countries but never accurate for the Global South
questions.explanation: 'Always more mythological than accurate, but it has become increasingly misleading as platform consolidation has accelerated.'
questions.hint: The discussant says the narrative was never accurate.

questions.prompt: What criticism do opponents of the term 'digital colonialism' make?
options: The term is too vague to be analytically useful
The term trivialises the history of material colonial violence
The term exaggerates the power of technology companies
The term focuses too narrowly on data while ignoring trade policy
questions.explanation: 'Critics of the term argue that it trivialises the history of material colonial violence.'
questions.hint: What do critics say the term does to colonial history?

questions.prompt: What structural dynamics do proponents of 'digital colonialism' say make the term apt?
options: Technological barriers, network effects, and venture capital flows
Extraction of value from peripheral regions to core ones, dependency relationships, suppression of local alternatives
Military enforcement, territorial occupation, and legal subjugation
Currency exchange rates, trade tariffs, and intellectual property theft
questions.explanation: 'Extraction of value from peripheral regions to core ones, dependency relationships, suppression of local alternatives — are genuinely analogous.'
questions.hint: Three dynamics are listed in em-dashes.

questions.prompt: According to the discussant, what happens when a user in Kenya engages with a social media platform?
options: They receive payment for the data they generate
They generate data monetised by corporations headquartered in California or Beijing, with the value not returning to their community
They benefit from advertising revenue shared with local publishers
They contribute to a global data commons governed by international law
questions.explanation: 'The value created by that engagement largely does not return to the community that generated it. The user receives a service; the platform receives a commodity.'
questions.hint: Who benefits from the exchange, and who does not?

questions.prompt: What does the academic say about the governance frameworks that users in Lagos or Manila are subject to?
options: They are designed for local contexts and adapted from international standards
They were developed by regional multi-stakeholder bodies
They are designed for regulatory environments and cultural sensibilities in the US or Europe, and cannot be meaningfully contested
They are more protective of user rights than European frameworks
questions.explanation: 'Designed for a different context — primarily for regulatory environments and cultural sensibilities in the United States or Europe. Users in other regions are subject to governance frameworks they did not shape and cannot meaningfully contest.'
questions.hint: For whom were these governance frameworks designed?

questions.prompt: What problem with content moderation does the discussant highlight?
options: Moderation is applied inconsistently due to political pressure from governments
African language content is moderated with far fewer resources than English, with potentially severe consequences
Moderation teams lack sufficient legal authority to remove harmful content
Over-moderation has silenced political dissent in Western countries
questions.explanation: 'Platforms that invest heavily in English-language moderation and much less in Swahili, Amharic, or Yoruba. The consequences in contexts of political violence or electoral disinformation can be severe.'
questions.hint: Which languages receive less moderation investment?

questions.prompt: What challenge does the academic describe for governments seeking digital sovereignty?
options: Their citizens do not want government control of internet infrastructure
The infrastructure of their citizens' digital lives is physically located elsewhere and governed by foreign law
They lack the technical expertise to build their own internet infrastructure
International law prohibits the nationalisation of digital infrastructure
questions.explanation: 'The infrastructure of its citizens' digital lives is physically located elsewhere and governed by foreign law.'
questions.hint: Where is the relevant infrastructure physically located?

questions.prompt: Why does the discussant caution against treating China's internet model as a model for the Global South?
options: China's internet infrastructure is too expensive for developing countries to replicate
China's internet sovereignty is exercised in the service of authoritarian control, not genuine governance participation
China's model relies on censorship technology that is technically obsolete
China's internet is closed to international trade and investment
questions.explanation: 'It is sovereignty, but it is exercised in the service of authoritarian control. What most states actually want is something different: genuine participation in the governance of shared infrastructure.'
questions.hint: What distinguishes what China does from what most states want?

questions.prompt: What has happened to proposals for digital trade agreements addressing data localisation and algorithmic accountability?
options: They have been adopted by major economies as binding international law
They are under active negotiation at the World Trade Organisation
They have stalled in the face of opposition from incumbent platforms and the states that host them
They have been rejected by Global South nations as favouring Western interests
questions.explanation: 'Proposals... have stalled in the face of opposition from both incumbent platform corporations and the states that host them.'

questions.prompt: How does the discussant say AI sharpens the colonial analogy?
options: AI companies are headquartered in former colonial powers
AI foundation models are trained on data from few languages and contexts, embedding cultural assumptions that favour dominant groups
AI automation is eliminating jobs in the Global South faster than in developed countries
AI research funding flows from developing to developed countries
questions.explanation: 'Trained predominantly on data from a small number of languages and cultural contexts... carry embedded assumptions about good writing, valid reasoning, and appropriate expression.'
questions.hint: What do the outputs of AI models carry that reflects colonial dynamics?

questions.prompt: What is the academic's point about the labour dimension of AI training?
options: AI training has created high-wage technical jobs in the Global South
Data annotation work is concentrated in wealthy countries near the headquarters of AI companies
Annotation and data labelling work is disproportionately done by workers in Kenya, the Philippines, and similar markets under criticised conditions
Governments in the Global South have successfully regulated AI training labour conditions
questions.explanation: 'The annotation, content filtering, and data labelling work... is disproportionately performed by workers in Kenya, the Philippines, and similar labour markets, under conditions that have attracted significant criticism.'
questions.hint: Who does the labelling work, and what has been said about conditions?

questions.prompt: What does the academic identify as the most promising policy response to digital colonialism?
options: Individual states passing strong data protection laws modelled on the GDPR
Mandatory open-sourcing of AI models trained on public data
Regional cooperation to build shared digital infrastructure and create negotiating leverage
A new United Nations treaty on digital sovereignty
questions.explanation: 'The most promising avenue is regional cooperation, building shared digital infrastructure at a scale that creates genuine negotiating leverage with incumbent platforms.'
questions.hint: What scale does the academic say is needed for leverage?

questions.prompt: What is the logic behind the academic's regional cooperation proposal?
options: Individual states have too little political will but regional bodies have more
No individual state in the Global South has sufficient market size to impose conditions, but a coordinated regional bloc might
Regional bodies can bypass incumbent platforms by building entirely separate internet infrastructure
Regional cooperation reduces the cost of digital infrastructure through economies of scale
questions.explanation: 'No individual state in the Global South has sufficient market size to impose conditions on hyperscale platforms, but a coordinated regional bloc might.'
questions.hint: What does market size have to do with leverage?

questions.prompt: What complication does the discussant raise regarding regional cooperation?
options: Regional cooperation requires agreement on a common language for governance
Regional digital infrastructure would require massive upfront investment that developing countries lack
It requires institutional trust and political coordination that is difficult to sustain, as even the EU has struggled
Regional blocs cannot legally challenge the jurisdiction of American corporations
questions.explanation: 'It requires a level of institutional trust and political coordination that is genuinely difficult to sustain. The European Union... has struggled to translate the ambitions of its digital regulations into enforceable outcomes at pace.'
questions.hint: What does the EU example illustrate?

questions.prompt: How does the discussant end the conversation?
options: By arguing that despair is the appropriate response given the scale of the challenges
By calling for a new international treaty on digital rights
By acknowledging the scale of the challenge while arguing this should lead to honesty rather than despair
By suggesting that individual consumer choices can make the biggest difference
questions.explanation: 'None of this should lead us to despair, but it should lead us to be honest about the scale of what we are asking for.'
questions.hint: The discussant closes with two contrasted injunctions.

cloze.template: I want to begin with what I think is an underappreciated structural feature of the contemporary internet: the degree to which its physical and commercial infrastructure is concentrated in a handful of states and corporations. When we talk about digital connectivity as a democratising force, we tend to focus on endpoints — the billions of people who now have access to the internet — and ignore the architecture of control that sits between those endpoints and the content they access. That's a useful corrective. The narrative of the internet as a flat, {{b1}} network was always more mythological than accurate, but it has become increasingly misleading as platform consolidation has accelerated. We are now in a situation where a small number of {{b2}} cloud providers host the majority of global internet traffic, and a similarly small number of platforms mediate the vast majority of human communication, commerce, and cultural production. The term 'digital colonialism' has attracted both enthusiastic adoption and significant scepticism. Critics of the term argue that it {{b3}} the history of material colonial violence. Proponents argue that the term is apt because the structural dynamics — extraction of value from peripheral regions to core ones, dependency relationships, suppression of local {{b4}} — are genuinely analogous. I find the concept analytically useful even while acknowledging its limitations. The extraction argument is the most concrete one. When a user in Kenya or Indonesia engages with a social media platform or a search engine, they generate data — behavioural data, preference data, relational data — that is processed and {{b5}} by corporations headquartered in California or, to a lesser extent, in Beijing. The value created by that engagement largely does not return to the community that generated it. The user receives a service; the platform receives a commodity. And the asymmetry goes deeper than data extraction. The terms of service, content {{b7}} policies, and algorithmic curation systems that govern the digital experience of a user in Lagos or Manila are designed for a different context — primarily for regulatory environments and cultural sensibilities in the United States or Europe. Users in other regions are subject to governance frameworks they did not shape and cannot meaningfully {{b6}}. Content moderation is a particularly acute case. There have been well-documented instances where content in African languages has been moderated — or not moderated — in ways that reflect the resource allocation decisions of platforms that invest heavily in English-language moderation and much less in Swahili, Amharic, or Yoruba. The consequences in contexts of political violence or electoral disinformation can be severe. The infrastructural dimension is equally important and perhaps more durable. The {{b8}} cable networks that carry the bulk of international internet traffic are largely owned by consortia that include the major platform companies. Data centre locations, content delivery networks, and domain name infrastructure are similarly concentrated. A government seeking to exercise genuine digital {{b9}} faces the difficulty that the infrastructure of its citizens' digital lives is physically located elsewhere and governed by foreign law. China's model is sometimes invoked here as an alternative — a state that has built its own parallel internet infrastructure and enforces its own governance norms. But I think we need to be careful about treating Chinese internet sovereignty as a model for the Global South. It is sovereignty, but it is exercised in the service of authoritarian control. What most states actually want is something different: genuine participation in the governance of shared infrastructure, not national enclosure. That governance question is where I think the most important work is happening — or rather, not happening. The Internet Governance Forum and similar multi-stakeholder bodies have produced valuable dialogue but limited enforceable outcomes. Proposals for a digital trade agreement that addresses data {{b10}}, algorithmic accountability, and the taxation of digital rents have stalled in the face of opposition from both incumbent platform corporations and the states that host them. There is also the question of artificial intelligence, which I think sharpens the colonial analogy considerably. The large language models and foundation models that are reshaping professional and creative labour globally are trained predominantly on data from a small number of languages and cultural contexts. Their outputs carry embedded {{b11}} about what constitutes good writing, valid reasoning, and appropriate expression — assumptions rooted in the dominant cultural contexts of the training data. And the labour dimension of AI training is itself a significant issue. The annotation, content filtering, and data {{b12}} work that makes large AI models functional is disproportionately performed by workers in Kenya, the Philippines, and similar labour markets, under conditions that have attracted significant criticism from labour researchers and advocacy organisations. The value chain of AI is global; the {{b13}} are not. Given all of this, what does a serious policy response look like? I have seen proposals ranging from data sovereignty frameworks to mandatory open licensing of AI models trained on public data, to reformed international tax arrangements for digital services. Each faces significant implementation challenges. I wonder sometimes whether the reforms most needed are structural — changes to the ownership and governance of infrastructure — rather than regulatory. My own view is that the most promising avenue is regional cooperation, building shared digital infrastructure at a scale that creates genuine negotiating {{b14}} with incumbent platforms. The African Union's Digital Transformation Strategy and similar frameworks in Southeast Asia point in this direction. The underlying logic is that no individual state in the Global South has sufficient market size to impose conditions on hyperscale platforms, but a coordinated regional {{b15}} might. That logic is sound, but it requires a level of institutional trust and political coordination that is genuinely difficult to sustain. The European Union, despite its much greater economic weight, has struggled to translate the ambitions of its digital regulations into enforceable outcomes at pace. For regions with weaker institutional frameworks and greater external economic dependency, the challenge is correspondingly larger. None of this should lead us to despair, but it should lead us to be {{b16}} about the scale of what we are asking for.
